comments.text: Walsh paper has arxiv identifier 2404.03970 (comment to be removed once corrected)

For \(r=5\), I found the following example which I did not see listed on the page:\[ 2^5 17^6 + 7^{11} + 2^5 3^6 7^8 = 3^7 13^7. \]Numerically,\[ 772402208 + 1977326743 + 134481277728 = 137231006679. \]All four numbers are \(5\)-powerful, and the three summands have joint gcd \(1\). They are not pairwise coprime: the first and third summands share \(2\), and the second and third summands share \(7\). Equivalently,\[ 17\cdot 34^5 + 7\cdot 49^5 + 1029\cdot 42^5 = 1521\cdot 39^5. \]I am not claiming that this example is new; it may already be among unpublished examples. Also, please feel free to point out any mistake, especially regarding the interpretation of “coprime” here. I put a short reproducible verification here:KitaKen1 GitHub verification repository Since this example appears to be smaller than the displayed \(r=5\) example, it might be worth adding to the page as a smaller explicit \(r=5\) instance. AI usage disclosure: the numerical search and verification were carried out with assistance from Codex 5.5 using xhigh reasoning.

Correct. As the previous search was not exhaustive, it indeed did not generate the smallest example. For $r=4$, an exhaustive algorithm verified that if $a+b=c$ with $a,b,c$ $4$-powerful coprime, then $\max(a,b)>10^{14}$. It might be there are no solutions in this case. The latter being a strengthening of Fermat for exponent $4$. For $r=5$, I even do not dare to guess if there is a finite or infinite number of solutions. Having updated the algorithm (using the meet in the middle method), it is deduced that if $a+b+c=d$ has a solution where $d<10^{16}$ then it is either one of the two earlier found solutions, or $\gcd(a,b),\gcd(b,c),\gcd(a,c)>1$ while $\gcd(a,b,c)=1$. The two solutions found do not immediately indicate how to construct a general family.

We can at least show there are infinitely many such representations for $r\geq 6$. The idea is the following: Firstly, via binomial expansion, we may write $$(X+Y)^r=(X-Y)^r+\sum_{\substack{1\leq j\leq r\ j	ext{ odd}}}2 inom{r}{j}X^{r-j}Y^j.$$ This is a sum of positive terms when $X>Y>0$. The number of odd indices $j$ is $\lceil r/2ceil$, but we need exactly $r-2$ summands, so since $r\geq 6$, we can split the $j=3$ term. Put $t:= r-2-\lceil r/2ceil=\lfloor r/2floor-2\geq 1$. Choose distinct positive integers $v_1,\,\dots,\,v_t$ with $$v_i=i\quad(1\leq i<t),\qquad v_t=2 inom{r}{3}- inom{t}{2},$$ such that $\sum_{\ell=1}^{t}v_{\ell}=2 inom{r}{3}$, then $$(X+Y)^r=(X-Y)^r+\sum_{\substack{1\leq j\leq r\ j
eq 3	ext{ odd}}}2 inom{r}{j}X^{r-j}Y^j + \sum_{\ell=1}^{t}v_{\ell}X^{r-3}Y^3$$ has exactly $r-2$ positive summands on the RHS, namely $$(X-Y)^r,\qquad 2 inom{r}{j}X^{r-j} Y^j\quad (1\leq j
eq 3\leq r 	ext{ odd}),\qquad v_{\ell}X^{r-3}Y^3.$$ We can now force each summand to be $r$-powerful by choosing $X=q^r$, $Y=B^r$, where $B$ is divisible by every prime appearing in the coefficients $v_{\ell}$ and $2 inom{r}{j}$ for $j
eq 3$, and where $q>B$ is a prime not dividing $B$. With this choice, $X>Y>0$ and $\gcd(X,Y)=1$. Moreover, every non-first summand has the form $cX^a Y^b$, $b\geq 1$, and every prime divisor of $c$ already divides $B$. Hence, all coefficient primes appear with exponent at least $r$ through $Y=B^r$, while $q$ appears only through powers of $X=q^r$. Therefore, all summands are $r$-powerful. The total sum $(X+Y)^r$ is also an $r$-th power, hence $r$-powerful. Now, joint coprimality comes from separating the first summand. All non-first summands have prime divisors only from $XY$, whereas the first summand is $(X-Y)^r$. Since $\gcd(X,Y)=1$, we have $\gcd(X-Y,XY)=1$. Thus, no prime can divide all $r-2$ summands. Finally, there are infinitely many possible primes $q>B$. Varying $q$ gives infinitely many distinct such identities. This argument came from GPT-5.5 Pro, and can be read in greater detail here. I have also gotten Aristotle to autoformalise GPT's argument into Lean here. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The third question has been solved by P.G. Walsh in his preprint "A question of Erdös on 3-powerful numbers and an elliptic curve analogue of the Ankeny-Artin-Chowla conjecture". One can look it here: https://arxiv.org/html/2404.03970v1
Notice that that paper refers to Nitaj and Cohn itself, but also concludes the same result indeed.
Cambie, do you think the finitude of solutions in the second question could be deduced by the n conjecture, that is, the abc conjecture for more than three terms?

At firt sight, no. More exactly: If Ramaekers's variant would have been true, the answer would have been yes. Since then $a_1+ \ldots +a_{s}=x$ for coprime $r$-powerful numbers $a_i, 1 \le i \le s\le r-2$ and $x$ implies that $\lvert x \rvert \le \lvert x \rvert^{(1+o(1))(s+1)/r}$, and thus $x$ is bounded. But by HKS25, we know that one is false, and the bounds are too weak to conclude. (or one needs additional things, like while the $a_i$ are coprime, they have pairwise large gcd's, for which I do not see any particular reason) For reference on the state of the art on the n conjecture, I had a look at [HKS25].
